expanded_description: Unlock powerful shortcuts for right triangles: when an altitude is drawn to the hypotenuse, three similar triangles emerge, creating elegant geometric mean relationships that solve for missing lengths instantly.

core_concept.explanation: Drawing an altitude from the right angle vertex to the hypotenuse splits the original triangle into two smaller right triangles. All three triangles are similar to each other by AA similarity, creating proportional relationships that simplify to geometric mean formulas.

image.alt: Right triangle ABC with right angle at C, altitude CD to hypotenuse AB, highlighting three overlapping similar triangles
image.caption: One altitude creates three similar right triangles: $\triangle ABC \sim \triangle ACD \sim \triangle CBD$

sections.introduction: The key to right triangle similarity lies in recognizing how a single altitude partitions the original figure. Once you see the three triangles, the rest of the section becomes a matter of matching corresponding parts.

key_concept.definition: In $\triangle ABC$ with right angle at $C$ and altitude $\overline{CD}$ to hypotenuse $\overline{AB}$: $\triangle ABC \sim \triangle ACD \sim \triangle CBD$.
key_concept.context: The right angles are congruent, and each smaller triangle shares an acute angle with the large triangle. By AA similarity, all three are similar. Corresponding vertices must be aligned correctly to set up proportions.

image.alt: Color-coded overlay of the three similar triangles with corresponding vertices labeled
image.caption: Vertex order matters: right angle $\leftrightarrow$ right angle, shared acute $\leftrightarrow$ shared acute

principles.statement: To set up correct proportions, list vertices so that: (1) right angles match, (2) shared acute angles match, (3) remaining angles match.
principles.application: Always write similarity statements before setting up ratios. Misaligned vertices are the #1 source of errors in right triangle similarity.
principles.example: If $\angle C = 90^\circ$ in $\triangle ABC$ and altitude $CD \perp AB$, then $C$ in the large triangle corresponds to $D$ in the small triangles. The similarity statements are $\triangle ABC \sim \triangle ACD$ and $\triangle ABC \sim \triangle CBD$.
principles.analogy: Like matching puzzle pieces by shape: the right-angle corner always matches the right-angle corner, and the $30^\circ$ corner always matches the $30^\circ$ corner.

worked_examples.title: Writing Similarity Statements from a Diagram
worked_examples.problem: Right triangle $XYZ$ has right angle at $Y$. Altitude $YW$ is drawn to hypotenuse $XZ$. Given $\angle X = 35^\circ$, write all valid similarity statements.

step_by_step.calculation: $\angle X = 35^\circ \Rightarrow \angle XYZ$ shares $\angle X$ with $\triangle XYW$. In $\triangle YWZ$, remaining angle $= 180 - 90 - 55 = 35^\circ$, so $\angle ZYW = 35^\circ$

worked_examples.concept_applied: AA similarity and vertex alignment in right triangle altitude configuration

sections.introduction: The altitude to the hypotenuse creates a special proportional relationship: its length is the geometric mean of the two hypotenuse segments it creates.

key_concept.definition: If altitude $h$ divides hypotenuse into segments $p$ and $q$, then $\dfrac{p}{h} = \dfrac{h}{q} \Rightarrow h = \sqrt{pq}$.
key_concept.context: This comes directly from the similarity $\triangle ACD \sim \triangle CBD$, where corresponding legs are proportional. The altitude is the 'mean proportional' between the two segments.

image.alt: Right triangle with altitude labeled h, hypotenuse segments labeled p and q
image.caption: The altitude is the geometric mean of the hypotenuse segments

principles.statement: From $\triangle ACD \sim \triangle CBD$, the ratio of corresponding sides gives $\dfrac{AD}{CD} = \dfrac{CD}{BD}$, which simplifies to $CD^2 = AD \cdot BD$.
principles.application: Use $h = \sqrt{pq}$ whenever you know the two hypotenuse segments and need the altitude length, or vice versa.
principles.example: If hypotenuse segments are 4 and 9, altitude $h = \sqrt{4 \cdot 9} = \sqrt{36} = 6$.
principles.analogy: Like a bridge support: the height of the pier is mathematically balanced between the two spans it connects.

worked_examples.title: Finding Altitude from Hypotenuse Segments
worked_examples.problem: In right $\triangle RST$, altitude $SU$ to hypotenuse $RT$ divides it into $RU = 5$ and $UT = 13$. Find $SU$.

step_by_step.explanation: Identify configuration: altitude to hypotenuse creates geometric mean relationship

sections.introduction: Each leg of the right triangle relates to the whole hypotenuse and its adjacent segment through another geometric mean relationship.

key_concept.definition: Each leg is the geometric mean of the hypotenuse and the segment of the hypotenuse adjacent to that leg: $a = \sqrt{c \cdot p}$ and $b = \sqrt{c \cdot q}$.
key_concept.context: Derived from $\triangle ABC \sim \triangle ACD$ and $\triangle ABC \sim \triangle CBD$. The leg is the mean proportional between the full hypotenuse and its neighboring segment.

image.alt: Right triangle showing leg a, hypotenuse c, and adjacent segment p
image.caption: Leg = $\sqrt{\text{hypotenuse} \times \text{adjacent segment}}$

principles.statement: For leg $a$, the adjacent segment is the one sharing a vertex with that leg. Never pair a leg with the opposite hypotenuse segment.
principles.application: When solving for a leg, first identify which hypotenuse segment touches that leg's base, then apply $\text{leg} = \sqrt{\text{hypotenuse} \cdot \text{adjacent segment}}$.
principles.example: If hypotenuse $c=20$ and segment adjacent to leg $a$ is $p=8$, then $a = \sqrt{20 \cdot 8} = \sqrt{160} = 4\sqrt{10}$.
principles.analogy: Like a shadow cast by a pole: the pole's height relates to the total ground distance and the specific shadow segment touching its base.

worked_examples.title: Finding a Leg Using the Hypotenuse
worked_examples.problem: In right $\triangle MNO$, altitude $NP$ to hypotenuse $MO$ creates segments $MP = 6$ and $PO = 10$. Find leg $MN$.

step_by_step.calculation: $MP = 6$ touches vertex $M$, so it's adjacent to $MN$

worked_examples.concept_applied: Geometric Mean (Leg) Theorem with hypotenuse reconstruction

sections.introduction: Choosing the right geometric mean formula depends on what's given and what's unknown. Use this decision framework to navigate right triangle altitude problems efficiently.

classifications.value: When you know both hypotenuse segments and need the altitude, or vice versa
characteristics: Involves only the altitude and the two split segments
Does not require the full hypotenuse or leg lengths
classifications.behavior: Look for the altitude labeled $h$ and the two pieces of the hypotenuse it creates. Plug into $h^2 = p \cdot q$.

examples.explanation: Given altitude $h=12$ and one segment $p=8$, find $q$: $12^2 = 8q \Rightarrow 144 = 8q \Rightarrow q = 18$.

image.alt: Diagram highlighting altitude and two hypotenuse segments
image.caption: Isolate the unknown using $h^2 = pq$

classifications.value: When you know the full hypotenuse and one segment, and need a leg
characteristics: Relates leg, hypotenuse, and adjacent segment
Requires reconstructing full hypotenuse if only segments are given
classifications.behavior: Identify the leg in question, find its touching segment on the hypotenuse, add segments if needed to get full hypotenuse, apply formula.

process_flow.description: Mark the right angle, altitude, hypotenuse, both segments, and both legs. Assign variables to unknowns.
process_flow.result: Clear visual map of all relationships

process_flow.description: Are you solving for altitude, leg, or hypotenuse segment? This determines which theorem to use.

process_flow.process: Set up and solve the equation
process_flow.description: Plug known values into the correct formula. Isolate the unknown. Simplify radicals.
Check that $a^2 + b^2 = c^2$ holds with your calculated values to confirm consistency.

categories.description: Right triangle altitude problems have predictable error patterns. Recognize and avoid them.
categories.detailed_mechanism: Most errors stem from: (1) pairing a leg with the wrong hypotenuse segment, (2) forgetting to add segments to find the full hypotenuse before using the leg theorem, (3) using the altitude theorem when legs are involved.

examples.explanation: If finding leg $a$, using segment $q$ (opposite) instead of $p$ (adjacent) gives an incorrect result. Always trace the leg down to the hypotenuse to find its neighbor.

image.alt: Diagram with checkmark on correct adjacent segment and X on wrong segment
image.caption: Match each leg to the hypotenuse segment sharing its base vertex

examples.explanation: Given segments 3 and 12, finding a leg requires $c = 3+12=15$ first. Using $\sqrt{3 \cdot 12}$ incorrectly applies the altitude formula to a leg problem.
examples.note: Leg theorem always uses the FULL hypotenuse, not just a segment.

summary: When an altitude is drawn to the hypotenuse of a right triangle, it creates three similar right triangles. This configuration yields two powerful geometric mean shortcuts: (1) The altitude is the geometric mean of the two hypotenuse segments ($h = \sqrt{pq}$), and (2) Each leg is the geometric mean of the full hypotenuse and its adjacent segment ($\text{leg} = \sqrt{c \cdot p}$). Success requires precise vertex alignment, correct identification of adjacent vs. opposite segments, and remembering that the leg theorem requires the full hypotenuse length. Always verify solutions with the Pythagorean Theorem when possible.
